mnemonics.title: Data Sharing Attributes
mnemonics.content: P-S-F-R-S: Private, Shared, Firstprivate, Reduction, Shared (default) - helps recall main data scope clauses and their purposes in OpenMP parallel regions.

mnemonics.title: Schedule Types
mnemonics.content: S-D-G-A-R: Static, Dynamic, Guided, Auto, Runtime - remember the order helps recall scheduling options from simplest to most dynamic.

mnemonics.title: Synchronization Directives
mnemonics.content: C-A-B-O-N: Critical, Atomic, Barrier, Ordered, Nowait - key synchronization constructs for controlling thread execution and preventing race conditions.

acronyms.usage: API for shared-memory parallel programming in C, C++, and Fortran
Environment variable prefix for runtime configuration (e.g., OMP_NUM_THREADS)
Programming model where all threads execute the same program on different data portions
Vector parallelism within a single thread; can be combined with OpenMP
Memory architecture where access time depends on memory location relative to CPU

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: single thread (vertical line) forks into multiple parallel threads (branching lines) that execute simultaneously, then converge back (join) at synchronization point, continuing as single thread.

Represent a cache line as a horizontal box containing multiple variables. When Thread A writes to Var1 and Thread B writes to Var2 in the same cache line, show invalidation arrows bouncing between CPUs, causing performance penalty.

Show tree reduction: each thread computes partial sum, then pairs combine (Thread0+Thread1, Thread2+Thread3), then these combine, etc., forming a binary tree that converges to final result in O(log P) steps.

analogies.analogy: Think of parallel for like assigning seats in a stadium: static scheduling is like section-based assignment (each section gets fixed seats), dynamic is like ticket holders grabbing available seats as they arrive.

Critical section is like a single-occupancy bathroom - only one person can use it at a time, others must wait outside the door. The 'name' of critical is like having different bathrooms for different purposes.

Private variables are like personal notepads for each team member - each person has their own copy to write notes during a meeting, and these notes don't affect the main document until explicitly shared.
